title: Market Sizing — TAM / SAM / SOM (Phased: Software Platform + Hardware Upgrade Layer)

READ_THIS_FIRST.statement: This file contains NO market size number, and that is deliberate. It now sizes TWO layered opportunities instead of one, because the company itself now has two layers.
READ_THIS_FIRST.why: A defensible bottom-up market size requires the number of aquaculture operations in the target geography, their size distribution, and their existing spend (on inputs, on monitoring, and now on farm-management software or its substitutes — notebooks, memory, Facebook groups). We have none of those counts. The BFAR Philippine Fisheries Profile has them; we have not obtained it.
READ_THIS_FIRST.what_changed_since_v1: The company used to be a hardware/monitoring-service business, and this file sized only that. It is now a phased platform: a free-or-cheap software layer (Phase 1) that most fish farmers of any size can adopt with a smartphone and no capital, a premium subscription/marketplace layer (Phase 2) monetising the farms that outgrow manual tracking, and a hardware upgrade layer (Phase 3 — the original OmniDrone/IoT/PondTwin business) sold to commercial and enterprise operators who need automated monitoring. Each layer has a different addressable population, a different sales motion, and a different set of missing inputs. Collapsing them into one TAM would hide the fact that the software layer is much larger and much lower-friction than the hardware layer — that asymmetry is the whole reason for the pivot, and this file should make it legible rather than smoothing it over.
READ_THIS_FIRST.what_we_could_do_instead: We could take FAO's USD 312.8 billion global aquaculture output, apply an invented 'technology penetration rate,' and present a confident TAM for either layer. It would look professional. It would be meaningless, because the penetration rate would be pulled from nothing and the whole number would inherit its arbitrariness. This applies just as much to a 'percent of farmers who will use a free app' assumption as it did to the old 'percent who will buy a drone service' assumption — a fabricated conversion funnel is a fabricated market size wearing a SaaS costume.
READ_THIS_FIRST.what_this_file_does_instead: It gives the METHOD, the inputs required, and the sensitivity structure for both layers — so that the moment real data arrives (farmer discovery interviews, a BFAR operator count, or an actual Phase 1 cohort with measured activation and retention), the numbers can be computed honestly and defended under questioning.
READ_THIS_FIRST.when_to_fill_this_in: The software layer (Phase 1/2) can be partially estimated sooner than the hardware layer, because it does not require BFAR data to launch a pilot — a founder can start collecting real activation, retention, and conversion numbers from an actual free-tier cohort well before BVLOS or lake-cage counts are resolved. That is one of the structural advantages of leading with software: the sizing inputs become obtainable through the product itself, not only through external records requests. The hardware layer (Phase 3) remains blocked on BFAR/LLDA data and on farmer discovery exactly as before.

the_layering_logic.statement: The three phases are not three unrelated markets. They are three progressively narrower filters over the same underlying population of aquaculture operators, and the whole point of sequencing them this way is that each filter is easier to pass than the one before it.

the_funnel.who_qualifies: Anyone farming fish or shrimp who owns or can access a smartphone. No capital threshold, no minimum scale, no regulatory gate.
the_funnel.friction: Lowest. This is the layer the old hardware-first plan could never reach on its own economics.

the_funnel.who_qualifies: Operations that have outgrown manual tracking and have a recurring budget line, however small, for inputs or advice.
the_funnel.friction: Medium. Requires the operator to have generated enough Phase 1 usage data to see value in analytics, and to have a reason to pay (an owner to answer to, an audit to pass, a margin to defend).

the_funnel.layer: Phase 3 — IoT sensors / OmniDrone / PondTwin / enterprise dashboard
the_funnel.who_qualifies: Commercial or enterprise operators at the scale, capital, and response-capacity threshold analysed in the hardware section below (unchanged from the original sizing logic).
the_funnel.friction: Highest — exactly as demanding as the original hardware-first plan, because this is that plan, now arriving third instead of first.

the_layering_logic.why_this_matters_for_sizing: The Phase 1 population is necessarily a superset of the Phase 3 population — every hardware customer was, or could have been, a free-tier app user first. This means the software TAM is not a separate business from the hardware TAM; it is the outer ring of the same target, and the hardware SAM/SOM computed in the section below should in the mature state be read as a sub-segment of the software SAM, not as an independent figure. We keep them as separate sections in this file because the missing inputs, the unit economics, and the sales motion differ enough that merging them prematurely would produce a single number nobody could defend.
the_layering_logic.the_honest_caveat: This funnel logic is a plausible structure, not a measured one. No data yet exists on what fraction of Phase 1 free-tier users would ever reach the Phase 2 threshold, let alone the Phase 3 one. Treat the funnel as a hypothesis to falsify with real cohort data, the same way the personas in customer-personas.json are hypotheses rather than research.

layer_1_software_market.label: Phase 1 / Phase 2 — Aquaculture Farm-Management Software (App, Premium Analytics, Marketplace, Consultations)
layer_1_software_market.why_size_this_separately: This is now the primary go-to-market layer and, on structural grounds, the larger and lower-friction of the two markets: it requires no capital outlay from the farmer, no airspace regulator, no dock network, and no minimum operation size. A farmer with one backyard pond and a smartphone is inside this market on day one; the same farmer is outside the hardware market until they can afford aeration and outgrow manual logging.

sizing_methodology.chosen_approach: Bottom-up, from the count of fish/shrimp farming individuals and operations with smartphone or basic internet access, filtered by realistic activation and monetisation rates benchmarked against comparable agtech and farm-management software — once those benchmarks are actually gathered.
sizing_methodology.why_not_top_down: The same objection as for the hardware layer applies here, and arguably applies harder, because 'percent of farmers who would install a free app' is an even easier number to fabricate persuasively than 'percent who would buy a drone service.' A believable-sounding conversion funnel is still a fabrication if none of its rates have been measured.

formula.TAM: Global count of individuals and enterprises engaged in aquaculture farming x share reachable with a smartphone/mobile-internet-based product
formula.SAM: TAM filtered to geographies AquaVision can realistically reach with a Filipino/Southeast-Asian-first product (language, species relevance, regulatory simplicity for software) x share who are actively farming (not capture fisheries, not purely academic/hobbyist)
formula.SOM_phase1: SAM x realistic free-tier activation rate within the planning horizon, constrained by community/organic distribution capacity and content localisation effort
formula.SOM_phase2: SOM_phase1 x realistic free-to-paid conversion rate, constrained by the premium value proposition actually landing (predictive health, marketplace, consultations) and by the operator having a budget line to convert

required_inputs.input: Number of aquaculture operations in the Philippines by system type and scale, split by smallholder/first-time vs established/commercial
required_inputs.note: The same blocking gap as the hardware layer. It is the base population for both TAMs; the layers differ in what filters get applied on top of it, not in needing this count.

required_inputs.input: Smartphone ownership and mobile internet access/data-cost burden among Philippine fisherfolk and small-scale aquaculture households
required_inputs.source: DICT/PSA ICT household use surveys, or a targeted farmer-discovery survey
required_inputs.note: This is the single most important new input this layer introduces relative to the hardware sizing, and it does not yet exist anywhere in this repository.

required_inputs.input: Comparable freemium agtech/farm-management app benchmarks: activation rate, 30/90-day retention, free-to-paid conversion rate, ARPU
required_inputs.source: Published case studies or investor materials from comparable products (general farm-record apps, and, where verifiable, aquaculture-specific players — see competitive-landscape.json for the category)
required_inputs.note: We have not gathered a single comparable-product benchmark yet. Every SaaS or freemium funnel assumption used anywhere else in this repository (go-to-market.json, unit-economics.json) inherits from this same gap. Do not let a plausible-sounding percentage substitute for an actual comparable.

required_inputs.input: Willingness to pay for Phase 2 premium tier, marketplace take-rate, and consultation fees, measured rather than asserted
required_inputs.source: Farmer discovery interviews, ideally validated via an actual paid pilot cohort once Phase 1 has real users
required_inputs.note: Same discipline as the hardware layer's willingness-to-pay input: stated willingness in a survey is unreliable; an invoice someone actually paid is the only trustworthy signal.

required_inputs.input: Existing spend, if any, on farm-management software substitutes (paper logbooks, spreadsheets, WhatsApp/Facebook groups, paid bookkeeping help)
required_inputs.note: Almost certainly near zero in cash terms, which is the software-layer analogue of the hardware layer's 'near-zero existing monitoring spend' finding: we are not winning share of an existing software budget, we are creating a habit. That is a different and generally easier problem than creating a budget line, but it is still unmeasured.

required_inputs.input: Count of extension officers, cooperative networks, and farmer Facebook/community groups reachable for organic/community-led distribution
required_inputs.source: BFAR regional offices, cooperative federations, informal community mapping
required_inputs.note: This determines whether the low-CAC organic distribution thesis in go-to-market.json is realistic or aspirational.

what_is_in.segment: First-time and beginner smallholder pond/cage owners (Phase 1 primary acquisition target)
what_is_in.rationale: The segment the old hardware-first model explicitly deferred on affordability grounds. A free or near-free app has no capital gate, so this segment is now the top of the funnel rather than an excluded one. See customer-personas.json for the new first-time-pond-owner persona.

what_is_in.segment: Established smallholder operators (e.g., long-tenured cage operators)
what_is_in.rationale: Already farming, already candidates for the free tier even where they remain out of reach for hardware on affordability grounds.
The realistic Phase 2 paying segment — see Marissa in customer-personas.json — who convert from free usage to premium analytics once volume outgrows manual tracking.
Both a distribution channel and, potentially, a bulk/seat-based licensing customer for Phase 2.

what_is_out.rationale: Different problem, different customer — nothing to farm-manage.

what_is_out.segment: Farmers with no smartphone or internet access at all
what_is_out.rationale: Structurally excluded from a software-first product regardless of price. This is a real ceiling on the software TAM and should not be hand-waved away; it is also the reason cooperatives and extension officers remain a relevant channel even for the free tier — someone with connectivity can relay guidance to someone without it.

what_is_out.segment: RAS and other systems already served by enterprise precision-aquaculture software
what_is_out.rationale: Already instrumented and already software-served by incumbents with real capital and validation data — see competitive-landscape.json. We are not trying to displace an existing enterprise contract with a free tier.

market_definition.why_the_exclusions_matter: The connectivity ceiling is the software layer's equivalent of the hardware layer's scale threshold: it is the honest boundary that keeps the SAM credible rather than inflated to 'everyone who farms fish.'

beachhead_definition.segment: Filipino smallholder and first-time fish/shrimp farmers active in existing online farming communities (Facebook groups, cooperative messaging channels), plus commercial operators willing to pilot the premium tier.
why_this_one: Matches the existing hardware beachhead geography (Philippines), so learnings, language, and species content are reused rather than duplicated.
Organic/community-led distribution through existing farmer groups is plausibly low-CAC — though this is unmeasured and listed above as a blocking required input.

First-time farmers are, by definition, actively searching for guidance right now, which is the moment a free educational/AI-assistant product has the most obvious value — an easier activation moment than trying to displace an established workflow.
beachhead_definition.beachhead_sizing_status: NOT COMPUTED — blocked on the same BFAR operator counts as the hardware beachhead, plus the smartphone-penetration and comparable-app-benchmark inputs above. Unlike the hardware beachhead, a rough order-of-magnitude estimate could be attempted sooner using publicly available Philippine smartphone penetration statistics and a conservative activation assumption — but doing so before that data is gathered would repeat exactly the fabrication this file exists to avoid.

why_now: Aquaculture passed capture fisheries in 2022, and inland/smallholder-dense systems are the majority — the underlying population of potential app users grows structurally.

Smartphone and mobile-data costs have fallen enough across Southeast Asia to make a mobile-first product plausible for many smallholders, though the exact penetration rate for this specific population is unverified (see required_inputs above).
Freemium and community-led distribution playbooks are well established in adjacent developing-market agtech, which is borrowable methodology even before we have our own comparable benchmarks.

why_not_now.note: The honest counter-case, in the same spirit as the hardware section below.
arguments: Low digital literacy and intermittent connectivity in exactly the smallholder population we are targeting could mean the 'low-friction' software layer is less low-friction than it looks on a slide — see the social/technological notes in swot-porter.json.

A free tier with no monetisation path that actually converts is not a business, it is a cost centre; the Phase 1-to-Phase 2 conversion rate is entirely unmeasured and could be far lower than any comparable-product benchmark suggests once localised for this specific population.

Generic, crop-agnostic farm-management apps and, in some markets, aquaculture-specific platforms already exist (see competitive-landscape.json) and could add tilapia/milkfish/shrimp templates faster than we can build a defensible position, since software has an even lower replication barrier than hardware.

layer_2_hardware_market.label: Phase 3 — Smart Aquaculture Hardware Upgrade (IoT Sensors, OmniDrone, PondTwin, Enterprise Dashboard)
layer_2_hardware_market.reframing_note: This is the original hardware/monitoring-service market sizing from v1.0.0 of this file, preserved with its method and honesty intact. It is no longer the company's core market — it is now the Phase 3 upsell layer sold to commercial and enterprise operators who have outgrown Phase 1/2 and need automated monitoring. Nothing about the underlying analysis has changed; only its role in the company's narrative has.

sizing_methodology.chosen_approach: Bottom-up, from operator counts and per-operator willingness to pay, restricted to the subset of the software layer's population that clears the scale/response-capacity threshold analysed below.
sizing_methodology.why_not_top_down: Top-down sizing from industry output value requires a penetration assumption that carries the entire result. The number becomes a restatement of the assumption dressed as research. Bottom-up sizing can be wrong, but it can be checked — and a number that can be checked is worth more than a bigger number that cannot.

formula.TAM: SUM over all aquaculture operations globally of (annual revenue per operation from monitoring + dispensing services)
formula.SAM: SUM over operations in reachable geographies, in culture systems the platform physically suits (open-water pond, lake cage, coastal pen — NOT RAS, NOT deep offshore), that are above the minimum scale threshold
formula.SOM: SAM x realistic share within the planning horizon, constrained by dock network coverage, service capacity, and regulatory permission

required_inputs.input: Number of aquaculture operations in the Philippines by system type and scale
Cage counts by lake (Taal, Laguna de Bay, Buhi, Sampaloc, Bato)
Size distribution of operations — the median matters far more than the mean here, because a handful of large farms will dominate the mean and mislead the strategy
Current spend on water quality monitoring per operation
required_inputs.note: Likely near zero for most operators, which is the crux of the whole commercial problem: we are not taking share from an existing budget line, we are trying to create one. Creating a budget line is far harder than winning share of an existing one, and the plan must reflect that. Phase 1/2 usage data, once real, may itself become evidence for this input — an operator already paying for Phase 2 analytics has demonstrated a monitoring budget line exists before a hardware salesperson ever calls.
This is the existing budget line we CAN take share from. It may be the more important number than the monitoring spend.

required_inputs.input: Feed spend per operation per cycle
Willingness to pay, measured rather than asserted
required_inputs.source: Farmer discovery, ideally via a paid pilot rather than a survey question
required_inputs.note: Stated willingness to pay in agricultural surveys is notoriously inflated. The only reliable measurement is an invoice someone actually paid.

what_is_in.rationale: The core case. Stratification-driven risk, spatially distributed units, a shared water body, and no practical alternative monitoring method.

what_is_in.segment: Open brackish and freshwater pond culture
what_is_in.rationale: Large area, distributed units, real monitoring need. The largest segment by count.

what_is_in.segment: Coastal pen and near-shore marine cage
what_is_in.rationale: Physically viable for the platform; harder environment; later phase.

what_is_out.rationale: Indoor, already instrumented, and fixed sensors are strictly better than an aerial platform in a tank. We would lose, correctly.
Beyond the endurance and sea-state envelope of a small multirotor. This is a vessel or fixed-platform problem.

what_is_out.segment: Single small ponds below the scale threshold
what_is_out.rationale: A fixed buoy is cheaper and better. Selling into this segment would be extracting money for a worse solution, which is both wrong and unsustainable — and, under the new model, this segment is not lost revenue anyway, since it is exactly the Phase 1 free-tier population instead.
Different problem, different customer, and — for the surveillance applications — a different company than the one we want to be.

market_definition.why_the_exclusions_matter: Roughly half of what a lazy sizing would count is genuinely not our hardware market. Stating the exclusions up front is the difference between a credible SAM and an inflated one. Under the phased model, several of these exclusions (single small ponds, capital-constrained smallholders) are not lost customers at all — they are Phase 1/2 customers who were never going to be Phase 3 customers, and the business model no longer needs them to be.

scale_threshold_analysis.the_question: Below what operation size does a fixed sensor buoy beat the OmniDrone — and, one level up, below what operation size does a Phase 2 software subscription remain the ceiling rather than a stepping stone to Phase 3 hardware?
scale_threshold_analysis.why_it_decides_everything: This threshold sets the hardware SAM, and it now also sets the boundary between 'permanent Phase 2 customer' and 'Phase 3 upsell candidate.'

the_logic.buoy_economics: A fixed buoy covers one point at one or few depths, at a low capital cost, forever, with no labour. For a single small pond, this is close to unbeatable and we should say so.
the_logic.omnidrone_economics: One drone covers N stations x D depths x F frequency, but carries a much higher capital and operating cost. Its cost per observation falls as N, D, and F rise.
the_logic.the_crossover: Cost_per_observation_buoy x (N x D) vs Cost_per_observation_drone. The drone wins when the number of distinct observation points needed is large — which is precisely the lake cage array case and precisely not the single-pond case.
the_logic.the_asymmetry_that_favours_us: A buoy cannot profile a water column without a vertical string of sensors, which multiplies its cost by D. The drone gets depth almost for free — the winch is already there, and paying out another two metres costs seconds. Depth is where our economics break in our favour, and it is not a coincidence that depth is also where the killing happens.

scale_threshold_analysis.estimated_threshold: unknown — must be computed once real costs and real farm layouts are known
scale_threshold_analysis.honest_note: This analysis is the most useful thing in this section and it is currently qualitative. Making it quantitative should be a Phase 0 deliverable. It determines the hardware SAM, the pricing, and the sales qualification criteria all at once — and now it also determines which Phase 2 subscribers are worth a Phase 3 sales conversation and which are not.

beachhead_definition.segment: Philippine lake cage culture operators, tilapia and milkfish, at cooperative or commercial scale
why_this_one: The failure mode the platform is built to see (stratification-driven kills) is documented, recurring, and expensive there.
Operators are geographically concentrated around a small number of lakes, which makes a dock-based service network viable — a decisive operational advantage over dispersed pond geographies.
Cage arrays produce exactly the high N x D x F profile where our economics beat a buoy.
Existing cooperatives provide an aggregation layer that solves the smallholder affordability problem without requiring us to solve it.
Local knowledge of Taal's volcanic sulfur chemistry is defensible against imported generic products.

Under the phased model, this beachhead is now reached warm rather than cold — ideally via operators who are already Phase 1/2 software users, which should lower acquisition cost and de-risk the sale relative to a pure cold hardware pitch. This is a hoped-for benefit of sequencing, not yet a demonstrated one.
beachhead_definition.beachhead_sizing_status: NOT COMPUTED — blocked on lake cage counts. This is the single highest-value missing datum in the repository, and it is obtainable with a phone call and a records request.

sequence.new_risk: none — this is the design case
Dispersed geography breaks the dock network economics; salinity and benthic species change the sensing value.
Regulatory re-approval per country, and local competition.

expansion_path.note: Each step is a real re-derisking, not a copy-paste. Business plans that show a smooth regional expansion curve are describing a spreadsheet, not a company. This expansion path is now nested inside a larger one: Phase 1 software can plausibly expand regionally faster and cheaper than Phase 3 hardware, since it carries none of the per-country regulatory re-approval burden — the software layer could be operating in Vietnam or Indonesia well before a drone is certified to fly there.

why_now: Aquaculture passed capture fisheries in 2022 — the instrumentable share of the sector is now the majority.
UAV inland-water retrieval literature matured through 2024-2025, so the science is borrowable rather than inventable.
Edge inference at a few watts became practical.
Digital twin aquaculture moved from proposal to demonstrated case study.

why_not_now.note: The honest counter-case, included because a founder who cannot argue the opposite side has not thought about it.
arguments: BVLOS is not authorised, and the unit economics depend on it. We may be two years early to our own hardware business model — which is exactly why hardware is no longer the first thing we ask the market to fund.

Smallholder willingness to pay for hardware may simply not exist at any price we can offer, in which case that market waits for a public programme rather than a startup — but this no longer blocks the company overall, because Phase 1/2 do not depend on it.
Battery energy density remains the binding constraint on multirotor endurance and is improving slowly. The mission profile may be marginal today and comfortable in three years.

chart_hint.rendering_note: The chart file should render the METHOD and the missing inputs for BOTH layers, not fabricated concentric circles or a single blended TAM. If a reviewer asks where the number is, the answer — 'we do not have the operator counts or the comparable-app benchmarks yet, and here is exactly what we need for each layer' — is a better answer than a wrong number, for the software layer just as much as for the hardware layer.
